steps.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Explain the legal status of statutory regulations.
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; compliance is mandatory and failure to comply is a criminal offense.
Electricians must know which documents are legally binding to avoid criminal prosecution.

In the electrical industry, statutory regulations are the 'Law of the Land.' These are acts and regulations passed by Parliament that everyone must follow. Because they are legal requirements, compliance is mandatory, not optional.

If an electrician or an employer fails to comply with a statutory regulation, they are committing a criminal offense. This can lead to prosecution in a court of law, resulting in heavy fines or even imprisonment. Think of these as the absolute 'must-do' rules that ensure safety and order across all workplaces. They set the legal framework that protects workers and the public from harm.

steps.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Explain the role of non-statutory guidance and codes of practice.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are guidance documents and codes of practice that describe 'best practice' but are not laws themselves.
While not law, these documents are often used in court to prove that a person has met their legal obligations.

Non-statutory regulations are often called 'The Professional's Rulebook.' Unlike statutory laws, these documents are not passed by Parliament and are not laws in themselves. Instead, they are guidance documents and 'Codes of Practice' that describe the best way to carry out work safely and effectively.

While you cannot be arrested simply for not following a non-statutory guide, they are still vital. If an accident happens and you end up in court, these documents are used as evidence. If you can prove you followed the non-statutory guidance, it shows the court that you met your legal (statutory) duties. They provide the technical 'how-to' for the industry.
Explanation B

If you can prove you followed the non-statutory guidance, it shows the court that you met your legal (statutory) duties. They provide the technical 'how-to' for the industry.

steps.title: The Statutory List
steps.objective: Identify specific statutory regulations relevant to the electrical industry.
keyIdeas: Key statutory items include HASAWA, EAWR, ESQCR, PUWER, COSHH, CDM, and PPE regulations.
Learners must recognize these specific acronyms as the 'Acts' and 'Regulations' that govern the workplace.

You must recognize specific names and acronyms as statutory laws. The most important is HASAWA, which stands for the Health and Safety at Work Act. This is the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety. For electricians, the EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) is the primary legal duty to prevent injury or death from electricity.

Other statutory regulations include: ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations) which covers supply; PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) for tools; COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for chemicals; CDM (Construction Design and Management) for site safety; and PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) regulations. The Environmental Act and the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) are also statutory laws you must follow.
Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.

steps.title: The Non-Statutory List
steps.objective: Identify specific non-statutory guidance and codes of practice.
keyIdeas: Key non-statutory items include BS 7671 (The IET Wiring Regulations), the On-Site Guide, Guidance Notes, and the Unite Union Book.
The Wiring Regulations (BS 7671) are the primary technical standard for electricians, despite being non-statutory.

The most famous non-statutory document in our trade is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations (the 'Big Blue Book'). Even though it is the main technical standard for every installation, it is technically non-statutory guidance, not a law.

Other non-statutory items include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified pocket reference for small jobs, and various Guidance Notes that go into deep detail on specific topics like testing or earthing. The Unite Union Book and various industry Codes of Practice also fall into this category. These documents tell you the specific technical steps to take to ensure your work is up to the standard required by the industry.
Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.

steps.title: Regulatory Identification (AC1.1/1.2)
steps.objective: Differentiate between statutory and non-statutory items from a mixed list.
keyIdeas: The ability to sort documents like the Environmental Act (Statutory) from Codes of Practice (Non-statutory).
Exam success depends on correctly categorizing these documents as per the C&G 2365 range.

To work safely and pass your exams, you must be able to sort these documents into two piles. In the 'Statutory' pile, you place the legal Acts and Regulations like the Environmental Act, HASAWA, and EAWR. These are the mandatory laws that can lead to criminal prosecution if ignored.

In the 'Non-statutory' pile, you place the technical guides and codes, such as BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and Guidance Notes. While these are not laws, they are the industry-standard way to prove you are working safely. An electrician uses the non-statutory BS 7671 to ensure they are complying with the statutory EAWR. One is the 'how-to' (non-statutory), and the other is the 'must-do' (statutory).

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world site scenario.
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are starting a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you a folder containing four documents: the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs), and the On-Site Guide. You need to identify which are legally mandatory 'Acts' and which are technical 'Guidance'.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the terms 'Act' or 'Regulations' and check if they are passed by Parliament. HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) and EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) are both laws of the land. Failure to comply with these is a criminal offence that can lead to prosecution.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for British Standards (BS) or industry-specific handbooks. BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide provide the technical 'how-to' for the installation. While these are the industry standards and are used as evidence in court, they are not laws passed by Parliament.
Final Result: You have two piles. Pile A (Statutory) contains HASAWA and EAWR. Pile B (Non-statutory) contains BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide.
Takeaway: Statutory regulations are the mandatory 'must-do' laws; non-statutory regulations are the 'how-to' guidance used to prove you have met your legal obligations.

terms.simpleDefinition: Laws passed by Parliament that are mandatory; failing to follow them is a criminal offense.
terms.misconception: Thinking statutory regulations are just 'best practice' suggestions rather than legal requirements.

terms.simpleDefinition: Guidance documents and codes of practice that describe how to meet the standards required by law.
terms.misconception: Believing non-statutory means 'optional' or that you can ignore them without professional or legal consequence.

terms.term: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
terms.simpleDefinition: The primary statutory law covering occupational health and safety in the UK.
terms.misconception: Thinking it only applies to the boss; it actually places legal duties on every employee too.

terms.simpleDefinition: Statutory regulations that specifically focus on electrical safety to prevent injury or death.
terms.misconception: Confusing these legal requirements with the technical rules found in BS 7671.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main non-statutory technical standard for the design and installation of electrical systems.
terms.misconception: Thinking BS 7671 is a statutory law because it is so widely used in the industry.

terms.simpleDefinition: A non-statutory handbook designed for quick reference on-site for small-scale electrical installations.
terms.misconception: Thinking the On-Site Guide replaces the full BS 7671 regulations for complex jobs.

terms.simpleDefinition: Non-statutory documents that provide practical instructions on how to comply with the law.
terms.misconception: Assuming all safety-related documents are automatically statutory laws.

anchorPhrases: Statutory is the law; non-statutory is the guidance.
BS 7671 is the standard, but EAWR is the law.
Non-statutory guidance is used in court to prove you followed the law.
misconceptionTargets: Statutory regulations are just suggestions for good work.

teachChecks.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Explain the legal status of statutory regulations.
retrievalTextLines: In the electrical industry, statutory regulations are the 'Law of the Land.' These are acts and regulations passed by Parliament that everyone must follow. Because they are legal requirements, compliance is mandatory, not optional.

If an electrician or an employer fails to comply with a statutory regulation, they are committing a criminal offense. This can lead to prosecution in a court of law, resulting in heavy fines or even imprisonment. Think of these as the absolute 'must-do' rules that ensure safety and order across all workplaces. They set the legal framework that protects workers and the public from harm.
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the fact that these are laws passed by Parliament and carry criminal penalties.

teachChecks.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Explain the role of non-statutory guidance and codes of practice.
retrievalTextLines: Non-statutory regulations are often called 'The Professional's Rulebook.' Unlike statutory laws, these documents are not passed by Parliament and are not laws in themselves. Instead, they are guidance documents and 'Codes of Practice' that describe the best way to carry out work safely and effectively.

While you cannot be arrested simply for not following a non-statutory guide, they are still vital. If an accident happens and you end up in court, these documents are used as evidence. If you can prove you followed the non-statutory guidance, it shows the court that you met your legal (statutory) duties. They provide the technical 'how-to' for the industry.
teachChecks.hint: Remember that these are 'best practice' guides used to prove you have met legal requirements.

teachChecks.title: The Statutory List
teachChecks.objective: Identify specific statutory regulations relevant to the electrical industry.
retrievalTextLines: You must recognize specific names and acronyms as statutory laws. The most important is HASAWA, which stands for the Health and Safety at Work Act. This is the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety. For electricians, the EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) is the primary legal duty to prevent injury or death from electricity.

Other statutory regulations include: ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations) which covers supply; PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) for tools; COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for chemicals; CDM (Construction Design and Management) for site safety; and PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) regulations. The Environmental Act and the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) are also statutory laws you must follow.
teachChecks.hint: Look for terms like 'Act' or 'Regulations' and acronyms like EAWR and HASAWA.

teachChecks.title: The Non-Statutory List
teachChecks.objective: Identify specific non-statutory guidance and codes of practice.
retrievalTextLines: The most famous non-statutory document in our trade is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations (the 'Big Blue Book'). Even though it is the main technical standard for every installation, it is technically non-statutory guidance, not a law.

Other non-statutory items include the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified pocket reference for small jobs, and various Guidance Notes that go into deep detail on specific topics like testing or earthing. The Unite Union Book and various industry Codes of Practice also fall into this category. These documents tell you the specific technical steps to take to ensure your work is up to the standard required by the industry.
teachChecks.hint: BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide are the primary examples of non-statutory technical guidance.

teachChecks.title: Regulatory Identification (AC1.1/1.2)
teachChecks.objective: Differentiate between statutory and non-statutory items from a mixed list.
retrievalTextLines: To work safely and pass your exams, you must be able to sort these documents into two piles. In the 'Statutory' pile, you place the legal Acts and Regulations like the Environmental Act, HASAWA, and EAWR. These are the mandatory laws that can lead to criminal prosecution if ignored.

In the 'Non-statutory' pile, you place the technical guides and codes, such as BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and Guidance Notes. While these are not laws, they are the industry-standard way to prove you are working safely. An electrician uses the non-statutory BS 7671 to ensure they are complying with the statutory EAWR. One is the 'how-to' (non-statutory), and the other is the 'must-do' (statutory).
teachChecks.hint: Statutory equals 'Law/Act/Mandatory' while Non-statutory equals 'Guidance/Standard/Best Practice'.

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice: Sorting Site Documentation
guidedPractice.objective: To correctly categorize specific industry documents into statutory and non-statutory groups.
retrievalTextLines: Recall the 'Act' and 'Regulation' rule: If the document is an Act of Parliament or a specific Regulation (like EAWR), it is Statutory.
Recall the 'Guidance' rule: If it is a British Standard (BS), a Guide, or a Code of Practice, it is Non-statutory.
Scenario: You are organizing your site office. You have the following four items: The Environmental Act, PUWER, the Unite Union Book, and Guidance Note 3.
guidedPractice.questionText: Identify which two documents from the list are Statutory and which two are Non-statutory.
answerGuidance: Statutory: Environmental Act and PUWER. Non-statutory: Unite Union Book and Guidance Note 3.
guidedPractice.hint: Look for the words 'Act' or 'Regulations' for the statutory pile. The others are industry-specific guidance.

practice.title: Independent Practice: Legal Status of BS 7671
practice.objective: To explain the relationship between non-statutory guidance and statutory law.
retrievalTextLines: Statutory regulations like the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) are the law.
Non-statutory documents like BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs) provide the technical detail on how to comply with that law.
In a court of law, following non-statutory guidance is used as evidence that you have met your statutory obligations.
practice.questionText: Explain the professional relationship between the non-statutory BS 7671 and the statutory Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR).
answerGuidance: BS 7671 is the non-statutory technical standard used to prove that an electrician has complied with the statutory requirements of the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR).
practice.hint: Think about how one document is the 'law' and the other is the 'evidence' used in court to show you followed that law.

steps.title: Defining Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Explain the legal status of statutory regulations.
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; compliance is mandatory and failure to comply is a criminal offense.
Electricians must know which documents are legally binding to avoid criminal prosecution.

In the electrical industry, statutory regulations are the 'Law of the Land.' These are acts and regulations passed by Parliament that everyone must follow. Because they are legal requirements, compliance is mandatory, not optional.

If an electrician or an employer fails to comply with a statutory regulation, they are committing a criminal offense. This can lead to prosecution in a court of law, resulting in heavy fines or even imprisonment. Think of these as the absolute 'must-do' rules that ensure safety and order across all workplaces. They set the legal framework that protects workers and the public from harm.
Explanation A

steps.title: Defining Non-Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Explain the role of non-statutory guidance and codes of practice.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory regulations are guidance documents and codes of practice that describe 'best practice' but are not laws themselves.
While not law, these documents are often used in court to prove that a person has met their legal obligations.

Non-statutory regulations are often called 'The Professional's Rulebook.' Unlike statutory laws, these documents are not passed by Parliament and are not laws in themselves. Instead, they are guidance documents and 'Codes of Practice' that describe the best way to carry out work safely and effectively.

While you cannot be arrested simply for not following a non-statutory guide, they are still vital. If an accident happens and you end up in court, these documents are used as evidence. If you can prove you followed the non-statutory guidance, it shows the court that you met your legal (statutory) duties. They provide the technical 'how-to' for the industry.
Explanation B

steps.title: The Statutory List
steps.objective: Identify specific statutory regulations relevant to the electrical industry.
keyIdeas: Key statutory items include HASAWA, EAWR, ESQCR, PUWER, COSHH, CDM, and PPE regulations.
Learners must recognize these specific acronyms as the 'Acts' and 'Regulations' that govern the workplace.

You must recognize specific names and acronyms as statutory laws. The most important is HASAWA, which stands for the Health and Safety at Work Act. This is the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety. For electricians, the EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) is the primary legal duty to prevent injury or death from electricity.

Other statutory regulations include: ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations) which covers supply; PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) for tools; COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for chemicals; CDM (Construction Design and Management) for site safety; and PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) regulations. The Environmental Act and the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act) are also statutory laws you must follow.

The Wiring Regulations (BS 7671) are the primary technical standard for electricians, despite being non-statutory.

The most famous non-statutory document in our trade is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations (the 'Big Blue Book'). Even though it is the main technical standard for every installation, it is technically non-statutory guidance, not a law.

The Unite Union Book and various industry Codes of Practice also fall into this category. These documents tell you the specific technical steps to take to ensure your work is up to the standard required by the industry.

To work safely and pass your exams, you must be able to sort these documents into two piles. In the 'Statutory' pile, you place the legal Acts and Regulations like the Environmental Act, HASAWA, and EAWR. These are the mandatory laws that can lead to criminal prosecution if ignored.

In the 'Non-statutory' pile, you place the technical guides and codes, such as BS 7671, the On-Site Guide, and Guidance Notes. While these are not laws, they are the industry-standard way to prove you are working safely. An electrician uses the non-statutory BS 7671 to ensure they are complying with the statutory EAWR. One is the 'how-to' (non-statutory), and the other is the 'must-do' (statutory).
Teach the core idea: 203.LO1.AC1.1.

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way statutory and non-statutory documents work together during an electrical installation?

steps.title: Worked Example: Categorising Site Documentation
steps.objective: To correctly distinguish between statutory laws and non-statutory guidance in a real-world site scenario.
keyTerms: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
keyIdeas: Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are starting a new commercial installation. Your supervisor hands you a folder containing four documents: the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), BS 7671 (The Wiring Regs), and the On-Site Guide. You need to identify which are legally mandatory 'Acts' and which are technical 'Guidance'.

Step 1: Identify the Statutory Regulations. Look for the terms 'Act' or 'Regulations' and check if they are passed by Parliament. HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act) and EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) are both laws of the land. Failure to comply with these is a criminal offence that can lead to prosecution.

Step 2: Identify the Non-statutory Guidance. Look for British Standards (BS) or industry-specific handbooks. BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide provide the technical 'how-to' for the installation. While these are the industry standards and are used as evidence in court, they are not laws passed by Parliament.
Final Result: You have two piles. Pile A (Statutory) contains HASAWA and EAWR. Pile B (Non-statutory) contains BS 7671 and the On-Site Guide.
Takeaway: Statutory regulations are the mandatory 'must-do' laws; non-statutory regulations are the 'how-to' guidance used to prove you have met your legal obligations.

spacedReview.objective: Recall the primary piece of legislation governing UK workplace safety as a foundation for specific electrical regulations.
retrievalTextLines: In your earlier health and safety units, we established that all work activities are governed by a legal framework.
There is one 'umbrella' Act of Parliament that sets out the general duties for employers, employees, and the self-employed.
Understanding that this is a 'Statutory' requirement is the first step in categorizing industry documents.
spacedReview.questionText: What is the full name of the primary statutory Act that governs health and safety in all UK workplaces?
answerGuidance: The Health and Safety at Work Act 1974.
This is a Statutory Regulation, meaning it is the law of the land.
